1.short: The human body works best when it follows a regular daily rhythm. Just like school follows a timetable for learning, the body also functions best when meals occur at consistent times each day.
1.full: Regular meal timing supports digestion, energy balance, and concentration. When students eat meals at predictable times, the body prepares for digestion, hunger signals become more balanced, and energy levels remain steady throughout the day. Irregular eating habits—such as skipping meals, eating very late, or constant snacking—can disturb the body’s natural rhythm. This may lead to tiredness, poor digestion, sudden hunger, and overeating. Breakfast is especially important because it provides energy after the long overnight period without food. Balanced meals during the day help students stay alert in school, participate in activities, and maintain good health. Developing the habit of respecting mealtimes helps children understand how regular eating patterns support both physical health and learning ability.

2.short: Water is essential for life and for maintaining good health. The human body is made up of about 60–70% water, and every system in the body depends on it.
2.full: Even though hydration is not classified as a nutrient, it is just as important as food for keeping the body functioning properly. Drinking enough water helps students: stay active and energetic concentrate better in class regulate body temperature support digestion and body functions It is important to remember that thirst is not an early warning signal. By the time we feel thirsty, the body may already be slightly dehydrated. The water we lose through sweating, breathing, and urination needs to be replaced regularly. When children do not drink enough water, they may feel tired, dizzy, thirsty, or unable to concentrate. Developing the habit of drinking water regularly throughout the day helps maintain hydration and supports overall health.

3.short: Screens such as phones, tablets, computers, and televisions have become an important part of modern life. They help us learn, communicate, and entertain ourselves. However, spending too much time on screens can affect children’s physical health, mental well-being, and daily habits.
3.full: Excessive screen use often replaces activities that are important for healthy development, such as outdoor play, physical exercise, reading, and spending time with family and friends. When children remain absorbed in screens for long periods, they may become less active and have fewer opportunities for social interaction. Too much screen time can also affect the body. Looking at screens continuously can cause eye strain and headaches. Sitting for long periods may lead to poor posture and discomfort in the neck or back. Using screens late at night can disturb sleep because the light from screens interferes with the body’s natural sleep cycle. In addition, excessive digital exposure can affect concentration and emotional well-being. Constant switching between apps, games, and videos may reduce attention span. Online content may also expose children to material that is confusing or inappropriate without proper guidance. Developing healthy screen habits helps students maintain balance. When children learn to unplug regularly and recharge through physical activity, creativity, rest, and real-life interactions, they support their health, learning, and happiness.

4.short: Our bodies are designed to move. Regular physical activity helps children grow stronger, healthier, and more energetic. When students move their bodies through play, sports, exercise, or daily activities, they support both physical and mental well-being.
4.full: Staying active helps build strong muscles and bones, improves heart health, and increases energy levels. Physical activity also supports brain health by improving concentration, memory, and mood. Children who stay active often feel happier and more focused in school. Movement is important not only for burning energy but also for supporting the body at a deeper level. Physical activity improves blood circulation, allowing oxygen and nutrients to reach different parts of the body. It also strengthens the immune system and helps the body repair and grow. However, many children today spend long periods sitting while studying, watching television, or using digital devices. Too much sitting reduces movement and may affect overall health over time. Encouraging students to include movement throughout the day helps develop lifelong healthy habits. Even small activities such as walking, stretching, playing outdoors, or helping with simple tasks can make a meaningful difference.

5.short: Sleep is essential for children’s health, growth, and learning. Just as the body needs enough food and nutrients, it also needs enough sleep to function properly.
5.full: During sleep, the body repairs tissues, restores energy, and prepares the brain for the next day. Sleep also supports learning, memory, emotional balance, and physical development. Most school-age children need about 9–11 hours of sleep each night to stay healthy, active, and focused in school. A practical routine may include sleeping around 10:00 pm and waking around 6:00 am, and if needed, taking a short afternoon rest. Developing good sleep habits early in life helps children maintain energy, improve concentration, and support healthy growth.

6.short: Being neat and clean is an essential habit that helps students stay healthy, confident, and responsible. Good hygiene protects the body from germs that can cause illnesses such as infections, stomach problems, skin diseases, and colds. Clean habits such as bathing regularly, washing hands, brushing teeth, and wearing clean clothes help maintain physical health and personal comfort.
6.full: Cleanliness also helps students feel fresh and energetic throughout the day. When students are neat and organised, they often feel more confident at school and during social interactions. In addition to personal hygiene, keeping belongings and surroundings tidy is equally important. Organised students are better able to focus on learning because they can easily find their books, materials, and school supplies. Clean surroundings at home and school help create a healthy environment for everyone. Simple habits such as throwing waste in dustbins, keeping desks tidy, and maintaining clean study spaces contribute to a more pleasant and safe environment. Developing the habit of being neat, clean, and organised helps students build discipline, responsibility, and respect for themselves and others.

7.short: Health is not only about the body; it also includes the mind and emotions. Emotional and mental wellbeing influence how we think, feel, and interact with others.
7.full: Children experience many emotions every day—such as happiness, excitement, sadness, frustration, or worry. These emotions are natural responses to experiences. Learning to understand and manage emotions helps children respond to situations calmly and make thoughtful decisions. The brain plays an important role in emotional wellbeing. It helps us recognise emotions, process experiences, and guide our behaviour. The brain also releases certain chemical signals and hormones that influence how we feel. For example, the hormone cortisol helps the body respond to challenges or stress. In small amounts, cortisol helps us stay alert and focused. However, when stress continues for a long time, cortisol levels can remain high, which may affect sleep, mood, concentration, and overall health. Developing emotional awareness and learning simple ways to manage stress helps children stay balanced, build confidence, and maintain positive relationships. Practicing habits such as recognising emotions, calming the mind, thinking positively, and supporting others helps strengthen emotional and mental wellbeing. This habit supports the principle of balance. Balance means caring for both the body and the mind. Just as nutritious food, physical activity, and sleep support physical health, emotional awareness and stress management support mental health.

8.short: Many foods we eat every day contain carbohydrates. Carbohydrates are the main source of energy for the body, which is why they are often called energy-giving foods.
8.full:
When we eat foods containing carbohydrates, the body breaks them down into glucose (blood sugar). Glucose is used by the body to: • provide energy for thinking, playing, and learning
• support muscles during physical activity
• store energy for later use Even when we are not eating, the body can still obtain energy. It can use stored sugar (glycogen) in the liver, stored body fat, or make glucose from other nutrients such as protein. However, some foods—especially sugary or highly refined carbohydrate foods—can cause blood sugar levels to rise quickly. This may be followed by a sudden drop in energy. Choosing the right types of carbohydrates, such as whole grains and less processed foods, helps maintain steady energy levels throughout the day. This habit encourages students to choose better carbohydrate foods and combine them with other foods to support balanced meals and steady energy.

9.short: Protein is an essential nutrient that helps the body grow, repair tissues, and stay healthy. About 20% of the human body is made up of protein, which shows how important it is for body structure and function.
9.full:
Protein supports several important functions in the body: • building and maintaining muscles
• repairing tissues that wear down during daily activity
• supporting growth during childhood and adolescence
• helping produce enzymes and hormones
• strengthening the immune system Proteins in food are made of small units called amino acids, often described as the building blocks of protein. There are about 20 amino acids, and 9 of them are essential, meaning the body cannot produce them and must obtain them from food. Protein can come from both plant foods and animal foods. Animal foods often contain complete proteins, while plant foods may provide different amino acids that complement each other when eaten together. Traditional meals such as dal-rice, roti-dal, khichdi, and idli-sambar combine cereals and pulses, improving the quality of protein in the meal. Protein-rich foods also help people feel full for longer, which can reduce frequent hunger and support balanced eating habits. This habit encourages students to include protein regularly in their meals and snacks to support healthy growth and strength.

10.short: Fats are an essential nutrient in our diet. While many people think of fats only as a source of energy, they also play several important roles in the body. Fats help support brain development, protect organs, help absorb important vitamins, and provide essential fatty acids that the body cannot produce on its own.
10.full: Fats provide more energy per gram than carbohydrates or protein, making them the most energy-dense nutrient. Even small amounts of fat can make meals more satisfying and improve flavour and texture. Certain fats, especially omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids, support brain function, growth, and overall health. At the same time, some types of fats—such as trans fats found in processed foods—can harm health and should be avoided. Learning to choose healthy fats helps students understand which foods naturally contain fats, how fats support important body functions, which fats are beneficial for health, which fats should be limited or avoided, and how to include healthy fats in balanced meals This habit supports the principles of balance and moderation, encouraging students to include healthy fats while avoiding harmful fats.

11.short: The gut, also known as the digestive tract, includes organs from the stomach to the small intestine and is responsible for breaking down food, absorbing nutrients, and removing waste from the body.
11.full: Inside the digestive system lives a large community of helpful microorganisms called gut bacteria. Together, they form the gut microbiome. These microorganisms help the body digest food, absorb nutrients, and protect against illness. A healthy gut helps us: digest food properly absorb essential nutrients maintain regular bowel movements strengthen immunity stay energetic and focused The gut and the brain are closely connected through what is called the gut–brain axis, where signals travel between the digestive system and the brain. Learning about gut-friendly foods helps us understand how simple everyday food choices can support digestion, overall health, and wellbeing. A gut-friendly diet includes: fibre-rich foods, probiotics and fermented foods, prebiotic foods that feed beneficial bacteria, and sufficient water. These help support digestion, maintain healthy gut bacteria, and promote overall gut health. It is therefore important that we include adequate quantities of gut-friendly foods in our diet.

12.short: Vitamins and minerals are tiny nutrients that our body needs in small amounts, but they are essential for growth, energy, and good health. Children need these nutrients every day to support their physical development, learning ability, and immunity.
12.full: Vitamins and minerals help the body perform many important functions. They help build strong bones and teeth, keep eyes healthy, support brain development, and help the body fight infections. They also help release energy from food, support muscle function, and help wounds heal. Because the body needs only small amounts of these nutrients, they are often called micronutrients. However, even though they are needed in small quantities, they are very important for maintaining good health. Children who do not get enough vitamins and minerals may feel tired, fall sick more often, or experience slower growth and development. A variety of foods provide these nutrients. Fruits, vegetables, whole grains, pulses, nuts, seeds, dairy foods, and certain animal foods all contribute important vitamins and minerals. Encouraging children to eat a colourful and varied diet helps ensure they receive many different nutrients. Eating fruits and vegetables of different colours, choosing whole grains and pulses, and spending time outdoors in sunlight for vitamin D are simple habits that support healthy growth. By developing the habit of eating foods rich in vitamins and minerals, students can strengthen their bodies, improve their ability to learn and play, and support long-term health.

13.short: Many foods and drinks that students consume every day contain sugar. Some foods naturally contain sugars, such as fruits and milk. However, many packaged foods and beverages contain added sugars, including sweets, biscuits, desserts, sugary drinks, and processed snacks.
13.full: Sugar is a type of carbohydrate that provides quick energy to the body. When we eat foods containing sugar, the body converts them into glucose (blood sugar), which enters the bloodstream and supplies energy for thinking, learning, playing, and other daily activities. However, foods and drinks high in added sugar can cause rapid increases in blood sugar levels. Frequent consumption of sugary foods and beverages may lead to excess calorie intake and reduce the consumption of more nutritious foods. Sugar also appears in many different forms—such as jaggery, honey, brown sugar, and white sugar—and may be present in packaged foods under different names. This habit supports the principle of control and moderation, encouraging students to limit added sugars and choose more natural, nutrient-rich foods.

14.short: Salt is commonly used to make food taste better, but eating too much salt can affect health over time. Salt contains sodium, a mineral that the body needs in small amounts to function properly. Sodium helps maintain fluid balance, supports nerve signals, and helps muscles—including the heart—work properly.
14.full: Although sodium is essential, the body requires only a small amount. Modern diets often contain more sodium than needed because many processed and packaged foods contain hidden salt. Regularly eating too much sodium may increase the risk of high blood pressure, heart disease, and kidney problems later in life. For children, developing the habit of eating foods with less salt is important for long-term health. Choosing fresh foods, limiting salty snacks, and using natural flavours such as herbs, spices, lemon, garlic, or ginger can help reduce salt intake without reducing taste. A balanced diet that includes fruits, vegetables, whole foods, and home-cooked meals naturally helps maintain a healthier balance between sodium and potassium. Potassium-rich foods such as bananas, spinach, lentils, and coconut water help counter the effects of excess sodium and support heart health. By learning to enjoy foods with less salt and recognising hidden sources of sodium, students can build lifelong habits that protect their health.

15.short: Eating is something we do every day, yet many meals are eaten quickly or while distracted by screens, conversations, or other activities. When we rush through meals, we may not notice how much we eat, how food tastes, or how our body feels.
15.full: Mindful eating encourages students to slow down and pay attention to the eating experience. This includes chewing food properly, noticing flavours and textures, recognising hunger and fullness signals, and appreciating the effort behind the food we eat. When meals are eaten calmly and with awareness, digestion improves and students are more likely to develop healthier eating habits. Mindful mealtimes also create opportunities to connect with family members, classmates, and friends. Practicing simple habits such as chewing slowly, pausing during meals, listening to hunger signals, sharing meals with others, and expressing gratitude helps students build a healthier relationship with food. By developing these small practices early in life, students can turn everyday meals into moments of nourishment, awareness, and connection.

16.short: Healthy eating is not only about eating enough food but also about eating a variety of foods. Different foods provide different nutrients that the body needs for growth, energy, and protection from diseases. Since no single food contains all the nutrients required by the body, it is important to include foods from different food groups in daily meals.
16.full: A varied diet helps the body receive carbohydrates for energy, proteins for growth and repair, fats for energy and body functions, and vitamins and minerals for protection and overall health. Meals that include different foods often appear colourful and diverse. For example, combining cereals, pulses, vegetables, fruits, and dairy foods in daily meals helps provide many nutrients. Food diversity is also considered a sign of better diet quality. When meals contain foods from many food groups, the chances of meeting the body’s nutrient needs become much higher. Encouraging students to eat a variety of foods helps them develop balanced eating habits. Rotating grains, trying different pulses, eating fruits and vegetables of different colours, and including nuts or dairy foods in meals are simple ways to improve dietary variety. By building the habit of including a variety of foods on their plate, students can support healthy growth, improve immunity, and maintain good health.

17.short: Food is one of the most valuable resources we have. Growing food requires water, soil, sunlight, labour, and energy. Farmers work hard to grow crops, and many people are involved in harvesting, transporting, selling, and cooking food before it reaches our plates.
17.full:
However, a large amount of food is wasted every day around the world. Experts estimate that about one-third of the food produced globally is never eaten, which means nearly 1.3 billion tonnes of food are wasted each year. Food waste happens at many stages: • at farms when crops are damaged or not harvested
• during storage and transport when food spoils
• in markets and restaurants when extra food is prepared
• in homes and schools when people take more food than they can eat Avoiding food waste is important because: • farmers and workers spend time and effort producing food
• many people still do not have enough food
• food production uses valuable natural resources
• food waste contributes to environmental problems Students can help reduce food waste by taking only what they need, finishing meals, storing food properly, and sharing extra food. Developing this habit encourages students to respect food and care for the environment.

18.short: Many foods we eat today come in packets, boxes, or bottles. These packaged foods contain food labels that provide important information such as ingredients, nutrients, serving size, and expiry date. Food labels act like a report card for food, created using scientific guidelines and food safety laws.
18.full: While the front of the package often promotes the product with attractive pictures and claims, the back or side of the package contains factual information that helps consumers understand what the food actually contains. Learning to decode food labels helps students: understand what ingredients are used in packaged foods identify foods that may contain high amounts of sugar, salt, or unhealthy fats compare similar foods and make healthier choices One useful habit is to check the first three ingredients on a food label. Ingredients are listed from the largest quantity to the smallest, so the first few ingredients tell us what the product mainly contains. When students learn to read food labels, they develop the ability to evaluate foods thoughtfully instead of relying only on packaging, advertisements, or taste preferences. Link to Principles of Healthy Diets This habit supports control and safety in food choices. Understanding labels also helps us. Developing this skill helps students become aware and responsible consumers who can make healthier choices in everyday life.

19.short: Food gives us energy, supports growth, and keeps the body healthy. However, food is beneficial only when it is safe to eat. If food becomes contaminated or mixed with harmful substances, it can cause illness instead of nourishment.
19.full:
Unsafe food may lead to food poisoning, stomach infections, and other health problems. Food can become unsafe due to germs, toxins, poor hygiene, or food adulteration. Germs can enter food through dirty hands, unclean utensils, contaminated water, flies, or improper storage. If food is not handled properly, these germs can multiply and make food unsafe. Sometimes harmful chemicals or substances may also be present in food. This can happen due to pesticides, spoiled food, or food adulteration, where inferior or harmful substances are mixed with food. Learning safe food practices helps students understand how to keep food clean and hygienic
• prevent germs from contaminating food, store and cook food safely, recognise adulterated or unsafe food, and choose food from reliable and safe sources.

20.short: Local and seasonal foods are foods that are grown in nearby regions and harvested during their natural growing season. Eating these foods helps people enjoy fresher meals, supports local farmers, and reduces environmental impact.
20.full: When food is grown locally, it usually reaches markets quickly after harvest. Because it does not travel long distances, it often stays fresher and more nutritious. Local foods also require less fuel, storage, and packaging compared to foods transported from far away. Seasonal foods grow naturally when the temperature, rainfall, and sunlight are suitable. These foods often match the body’s needs in different seasons—for example, cooling fruits in summer or nutrient-rich vegetables in winter. Choosing local and seasonal foods also helps support farmers and local communities. When people buy foods grown nearby, farmers can earn a stable income and continue sustainable farming practices. Developing this habit encourages students to appreciate traditional foods, understand seasonal eating patterns, and make healthier food choices.

21.short: Eating a variety of foods is important, but it is equally important to balance your plate. A balanced plate means including foods from different food groups in the right proportions so that the body receives all the nutrients it needs.
21.full:
Our body requires nutrients such as energy, protein, vitamins, minerals, and healthy fats. No single food provides all these nutrients. Therefore, we need to include different foods in our meals. A balanced plate usually includes: • Cereals and grains such as rice, roti, wheat, or millets for energy
• Protein foods such as pulses, beans, eggs, milk, or curd to support growth and repair
• Vegetables and fruits for vitamins, minerals, and fibre
• Healthy fats such as nuts, seeds, oil, or ghee in small amounts
• Water to help the body function properly Children who eat balanced meals often have more energy to study, play, and stay active. National nutrition guidelines such as the ICMR–NIN “My Plate of the Day” recommend filling half the plate with fruits and vegetables, one quarter with cereals or millets, and one quarter with protein-rich foods. This simple idea helps ensure that meals provide a balanced supply of nutrients. Learning to balance the plate helps students develop healthy eating habits for life.

22.short: Understanding how the human body works helps children become aware of their health and well-being. Every day, the body performs many processes that allow us to breathe, move, think, digest food, and respond to the world around us. These processes often happen automatically, but they depend on the choices we make about food, movement, rest, and our environment.
22.full: Children experience signals from their body throughout the day—such as hunger, thirst, tiredness, energy, discomfort, or pain. Learning how these signals relate to body functions helps students understand what their body needs to stay healthy. This insight introduces students to simple ideas about how the body works. They explore how breathing supplies oxygen, how the nervous system helps the body respond to the world, how digestion and emotions are connected, how food provides energy, and how posture and daily habits influence the body. Students also learn that the body communicates through signals such as hunger, thirst, fatigue, and pain. Recognizing and responding to these signals helps people care for their health. Developing awareness of the body encourages students to make thoughtful choices that support lifelong well-being.

23.short: All living things depend on nature for survival. The air we breathe, the water we drink, and the food we eat come from natural systems. These resources support life on Earth and allow the human body to function properly.
23.full: Air provides oxygen that the body needs to produce energy. Water supports many processes in the body, including digestion, circulation, and temperature regulation. Food provides nutrients and energy for growth and daily activities. Plants, animals, soil, water, and air are all part of interconnected natural systems. Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to grow and produce oxygen and food. Animals and humans depend on plants and other organisms for nourishment. However, natural systems can be affected by pollution, waste, and unsustainable use of resources. Understanding how nature sustains life helps students appreciate the importance of protecting air, water, soil, and ecosystems. This insight helps students understand that our health and health of our planet are closely connected, and that caring for nature supports the well-being of people and the planet.

24.short: Every meal begins with soil. Healthy soil supports the growth of crops that provide grains, vegetables, fruits, pulses, and nuts. Soil, seeds, water, sunlight, and living organisms work together to produce the food we eat.
24.full: Healthy soil contains minerals, organic matter, microorganisms, and nutrients that plants need to grow. However, in many parts of the world today, soils are losing organic matter, which reduces soil fertility and affects food production. When organic matter declines, soil may hold less water, plants may grow poorly, and farmers may rely more on chemical inputs. Seeds are also an important part of agriculture. Diverse seeds help farmers grow crops that can adapt to different climates, pests, and soil conditions. Pollinators such as bees, butterflies, birds, and other insects help plants reproduce by transferring pollen between flowers. Without pollinators, many crops would not produce fruits and seeds. Healthy farming systems also depend on biodiversity. Different plants, insects, and soil organisms help maintain ecological balance, improve soil health, and support sustainable agriculture. This insight helps students understand how soil health, seeds, pollination, and biodiversity support food production, the challenges facing farming systems today, and how small actions can support healthier ecosystems.

25.short: Food choices are shaped by many factors. People do not choose food only because they are hungry or because it is nutritious. Foods are often chosen because they look attractive, smell pleasant, taste good, or have a satisfying texture.
25.full: Our senses, especially taste, smell, sight, and touch, play an important role in how we experience food. Colourful dishes, pleasant aromas, and interesting textures can make food more enjoyable and influence what people prefer to eat. Cooking and food preparation can also transform ingredients. For example, roasting vegetables may bring out their natural sweetness, while herbs and spices can enhance flavour and aroma. These changes can make nutritious foods such as vegetables, pulses, and whole grains more appealing. At the same time, some foods taste very strong because they contain large amounts of salt, sugar, fats, or flavour enhancers. These ingredients stimulate taste buds and the brain’s reward system, making such foods very tempting even when they provide fewer nutrients. Understanding how taste works and how food preparation affects flavour can help students appreciate healthy foods and make thoughtful food choices.

26.short: Knowing how to prepare food is an important life skill that supports healthy eating. While selecting good ingredients is important, the way food is handled and prepared also affects its nutrition, safety, digestibility, and taste.
26.full: Simple preparation practices such as soaking, sprouting, and cooking help make many foods easier for the body to use. For example, soaking pulses before cooking reduces cooking time and helps improve digestion. Sprouting seeds and pulses increases certain vitamins and improves nutrient absorption. Cooking also helps destroy harmful microbes and makes food safer to eat. Some foods can also be prepared without oil or heavy cooking. Fresh fruits, fruit juices, salads, sprouts, and lightly cooked foods provide natural vitamins, minerals, fibre, and water. Such simple preparations help keep food light, refreshing, and easier for the body to digest. In addition to cooking, small home-based practices such as growing microgreens can increase the availability of fresh and nutritious foods. Microgreens are young plants harvested shortly after germination and can be grown easily in small containers. By learning basic food preparation skills, students develop practical knowledge that helps them prepare simple, nutritious foods and understand how everyday cooking practices support health.

27.short: Health is shaped by the choices people make every day. Eating nutritious foods, staying active, maintaining hygiene, and making responsible lifestyle choices help the body stay strong and resilient.
27.full: Some diseases occur due to infections caused by microorganisms such as bacteria or viruses. Other health conditions develop slowly over time due to long-term lifestyle patterns. These conditions may not show symptoms immediately but can affect health later in life. Healthy living also involves maintaining strong immunity, caring for digestive health, avoiding harmful habits, and practicing safety in daily life. When students understand how daily habits influence health, they become more aware of the actions that protect their well-being and help prevent disease.

28.short: Human health is closely connected to the health of the environment. The air we breathe, the water we drink, the soil that grows our food, and the ecosystems that support life all influence our well-being.
28.full: Earlier insights explored different aspects of health and food systems: Insight 1: Understanding how our body works Insight 2: Nature sustains life through air, water, and food Insight 3: Soil, seeds, and biodiversity grow our food Insight 4: Taste and culture influence food choices Insight 5: Food preparation and cooking skills improve nutrition Insight 6: Healthy habits help prevent disease This final insight shows that all these ideas are connected. When we protect nature, support sustainable food systems, and reduce environmental harm, we also protect human health. Students learn that healthy environments support healthy people, and that everyone can contribute to protecting the planet.
